message.content:
Adversarial reviewer: REFUTE this candidate; default refuted=true unless solid. Read the real chia code at C:/bugBounty/targets/chia-blockchain first.

CANDIDATE: SSRF via attacker-published on-chain DataLayer mirror URLs (unvalidated URL fetched by subscribed node)
class: SSRF (CWE-918) severity: High
file: chia/data_layer/download_data.py:313
source: On-chain DataLayer mirror coin memo URLs. Any user can create a mirror coin: create_mirror_puzzle() = P2_PARENT.curry(1) (chia/wallet/db_wallet/db_wallet_puzzles.py:90) is a trivial puzzle anyone can spend into, and get_mirror_info (db_wallet_puzzles.py:97-110) extracts launcher_id and arbitrary url byte-strings from the CREATE_COIN memos with no validation. Mirror.decode_urls (chia/data_layer/data_layer_wallet.py:82) does url.decode('utf8') with no scheme/host checks.
sink: aiohttp ClientSession.get(server_info.url + '/' + filename, proxy=proxy_url) in http_download() at chia/data_layer/download_data.py:313-318
data_flow: DataLayer.periodically_manage_data() (data_layer.py:980) -> update_subscription() (data_layer.py:1076) -> update_subscriptions_from_wallet() (data_layer.py:953) calls wallet_rpc.dl_get_mirrors() and concatenates EVERY mirror's .urls into the subscription server list with only url.rstrip('/'). fetch_and_validate() (data_layer.py:607,628) iterates servers_info, calls insert_from_delta_file() -> download_file() (download_data.py:110) -> http_download() (download_data.py:298), which issues session.get(server_info.url + '/' + filename). The url is fully attacker-controlled (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data, http://127.0.0.1:8555/...). No scheme allowlist, host/IP filtering, or SSRF guard exists in this native path (urlparse/scheme checks appear only in the optional localhost-bound s3_plugin_service).
excerpt: (none)

LENS = REACHABILITY & CONTROL. Is the source truly reachable/controllable by an untrusted remote or low-priv actor in a default node config (peer connection accepted? RPC exposed/authless? handler actually registered)? Is the sink reached (not dead code, not gated behind operator-only config)? If trusted-only or unreachable, refuted=true.

is_real=true only if it survives against the real code. Provide exploit_sketch and adjusted_severity if real. Return ONLY the structured object.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

299	    target_filename_path: Path,
300	    filename: str,

302	    server_info: ServerInfo,

307	    """

309	    Raises exceptions on errors
310	    """
311	    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
312	        headers = {"accept-encoding": "gzip"}
313	        async with session.get(

315	            headers=headers,

320	            size = int(resp.headers.get("content-length", 0))

322	            if size > max_delta_file_size_bytes:
323	                raise MaxDeltaFileSizeExceededError(f"Maximum delta file size exceeded: {max_delta_file_size} MiB.")
324	            log.debug(f"Downloading delta file {filename}. Size {size} bytes.")

329	                with target_filename_path.open(mode="wb") as f:

97	def get_mirror_info(
98	    parent_puzzle: Program | SerializedProgram, parent_solution: Program | SerializedProgram
99	) -> tuple[bytes32, list[bytes]]:
100	    assert type(parent_puzzle) is type(parent_solution)
101	    _, conditions = run_with_cost(parent_puzzle, INFINITE_COST, parent_solution)
102	    for condition in conditions.as_iter():
103	        if (
104	            condition.first().as_python() == ConditionOpcode.CREATE_COIN
105	            and condition.at("rf").as_python() == create_mirror_puzzle().get_tree_hash()
106	        ):
107	            memos: list[bytes] = condition.at("rrrf").as_python()
108	            launcher_id = bytes32(memos[0])
109	            return launcher_id, [url for url in memos[1:]]
110	    raise ValueError("The provided puzzle and solution do not create a mirror coin")

content.text:
This confirms the critical reachability fact: once an operator subscribes to a store_id (via `dl_subscribe` RPC), the node automatically pulls ALL mirror URLs published on-chain for that store_id from the wallet (`update_subscriptions_from_wallet`), and any attacker can publish a mirror coin for any launcher_id. So the operator only chooses the store_id; the URLs are attacker-controlled on-chain data.

Let me confirm the DataLayer service is opt-in and check whether it runs by default, plus confirm there's no SSRF guard.

content.content:

content.text: The chain is fully confirmed. The vulnerability is real and exploitable. Let me verify the proxy_url default (whether proxy short-circuits the attacker URL) and confirm there's genuinely no scheme/IP validation anywhere in this path.

content.content:
117:    proxy_url: str | None,
135:                target_filename_path, filename, proxy_url, server_info, timeout, log, max_delta_file_size
181:    proxy_url: str | None,
207:                proxy_url=proxy_url,
301:    proxy_url: str | None,
317:            proxy=proxy_url,

content.text:
proxy_url is just an optional outbound proxy (None by default), it doesn't validate the target. The candidate is confirmed.

Assessment: This is a real SSRF. The DataLayer service is opt-in (a separate daemon, not running on a default full node), and the operator must `dl_subscribe` to a store_id. But the URLs fetched are NOT operator-supplied — they come from on-chain mirror coins that any blockchain participant can publish for any launcher_id (`create_mirror_puzzle` is a trivially-spendable `P2_PARENT.curry(1)`). The node automatically merges every published mirror URL into its server list and issues `session.get(url + "/" + filename)` with no scheme/host/IP validation and (by default) `allow_redirects=True`. So a remote, unprivileged attacker controls the fetch target on any node subscribed to a store the attacker can mirror.

Severity adjustment: It's a real blind/semi-blind SSRF, but reachability requires the operator to be running the optional DataLayer service AND subscribed to a relevant store. The response body is written to a file and validated as a delta, so exfiltration of the response is limited (blind-ish), though redirects and connection behavior still allow internal port-scanning / metadata-endpoint hits. I'll set adjusted severity to Medium-High; the candidate's High is defensible given cloud-metadata (169.254.169.254) reachability, but the opt-in service gating tempers it.

input.exploit_sketch: 1) Operator runs the (opt-in) DataLayer service and subscribes to some store_id S via the dl_subscribe RPC (operator picks the store, NOT the URLs). 2) Attacker, as any blockchain participant, spends a coin into create_mirror_puzzle() = P2_PARENT.curry(1) (trivially spendable by anyone) for launcher_id == S, encoding a malicious URL string in the CREATE_COIN memos (Mirror.encode_urls just utf8-encodes; decode_urls just utf8-decodes, no validation). 3) On the node, periodically_manage_data() -> update_subscription() -> update_subscriptions_from_wallet() calls wallet_rpc.dl_get_mirrors(S) and inserts EVERY on-chain mirror url (only url.rstrip('/')) into the subscriptions table (from_wallet=1) in data_store.py:1645. 4) fetch_and_validate() -> get_available_servers_for_store() returns those ServerInfo rows; download_file()/insert_from_delta_file() -> http_download() (download_data.py:313) issues aiohttp session.get(server_info.url + '/' + filename, proxy=proxy_url) with proxy_url=None by default and default allow_redirects=True. 5) Attacker sets url to e.g. http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data or http://127.0.0.1:<port> to force the node to make requests to internal/cloud-metadata endpoints. No scheme allowlist, host/IP blocklist, or SSRF guard exists in the native path (urlparse/scheme checks live only in the optional localhost-bound s3_plugin_service). Response is written to a delta file and size-capped, so it is largely blind, but it still enables internal port scanning, metadata-endpoint contact, and request smuggling via redirects.
input.reason: Verified against real code. SOURCE is genuinely untrusted-remote-controllable: create_mirror_puzzle() (db_wallet_puzzles.py:90) is P2_PARENT.curry(1), spendable by anyone, and get_mirror_info (line 97-110) plus Mirror.decode_urls (data_layer_wallet.py:82) extract URL bytes from CREATE_COIN memos with zero validation. The node does NOT require the operator to supply the URL: update_subscriptions_from_wallet (data_layer.py:953, data_store.py:1645) merges every on-chain mirror URL for a subscribed store_id into its server list, and update_subscription (line 1084) runs this automatically each manage_data_interval (default 60s) for all subscriptions. SINK is live, not dead: fetch_and_validate (line 607/624/628) iterates servers_info and http_download (download_data.py:313) does session.get(server_info.url + '/' + filename) with no validation, proxy=None default. The only gating is (a) the DataLayer service being enabled (opt-in, separate daemon) and (b) the operator having subscribed to a store the attacker can mirror — both realistic for any DataLayer-using node. This is a real, remotely-triggerable SSRF; the only thing tempering it from full-blown is the opt-in service and the blind-ish response handling.
